questions.question: What is SARSA in reinforcement learning?
options: An on-policy TD control algorithm that updates Q-values using the actual action taken (State-Action-Reward-State-Action)
questions.explanation: SARSA is an on-policy TD control method. It uses the five-tuple (S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) to update Q-values, where A_{t+1} is the action actually taken.

questions.question: What is the SARSA update rule?
questions.explanation: SARSA updates Q(s,a) toward the SARSA target R + γQ(s',a'), where a' is the action actually taken in state s' (following the current policy).

questions.question: Why is SARSA called an 'on-policy' method?
options: It learns about and improves the same policy that is being used to collect experience
It learns about one policy while following a different policy
It does not use a policy
It only works with deterministic policies
questions.explanation: SARSA is on-policy because it evaluates and improves the same policy it follows. The next action a' in the update is sampled from the current behavior policy.

questions.question: What is Q-Learning in reinforcement learning?
options: An off-policy TD control algorithm that updates Q-values using the maximum Q-value of the next state
questions.explanation: Q-Learning is an off-policy TD control algorithm. It updates Q(s,a) using the maximum over next actions: R + γ max_a' Q(s',a').

questions.question: What is the Q-Learning update rule?
options: Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[R + γ max_a' Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]
questions.explanation: Q-Learning uses the maximum Q-value of the next state as the target, regardless of which action is actually taken. This makes it off-policy.

questions.question: Why is Q-Learning called an 'off-policy' method?
options: It learns about the optimal policy while potentially following a different exploratory behavior policy
It learns about the same policy it follows
It requires a model of the environment
It only works with greedy policies
questions.explanation: Q-Learning is off-policy because its update target (max_a' Q) corresponds to the optimal policy, even if the agent is following an exploratory ε-greedy policy to collect experience.

questions.question: What is the key difference between SARSA and Q-Learning?
options: SARSA uses the actual next action (on-policy); Q-Learning uses the maximum Q-value (off-policy)
SARSA is model-based; Q-Learning is model-free
SARSA only works for episodic tasks
questions.explanation: SARSA's target uses the action actually taken (on-policy). Q-Learning's target uses max over actions (off-policy, optimistic). This makes Q-Learning more aggressive in learning optimal values.

questions.question: In which scenario might SARSA be preferred over Q-Learning?
options: When the agent must follow a fixed exploration policy during execution — SARSA learns the value of the policy actually being followed
When the state space is infinite
questions.explanation: SARSA learns the value of the policy being followed (including exploration). This is useful when the agent must use ε-greedy during deployment, as SARSA accounts for occasional bad actions from exploration.

questions.question: In which scenario might Q-Learning be preferred over SARSA?
options: When finding the optimal policy is the goal, regardless of the exploration strategy used during training
When the state space is small
questions.explanation: Q-Learning directly learns optimal Q-values independent of the exploration policy. It separates learning from behavior, making it more suitable when the final goal is optimal performance.

questions.question: What is 'Expected SARSA'?
options: A variant that uses the expected value under the current policy instead of sampling the next action: Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[R + γΣ_a' π(a'|s')Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]
questions.explanation: Expected SARSA uses the expected Q-value of the next state under the policy, rather than a single sample. This reduces variance compared to regular SARSA while remaining on-policy.

questions.question: What is an advantage of Expected SARSA over regular SARSA?
options: It has lower variance because it uses the expectation over actions rather than a single action sample
questions.explanation: By taking the expectation, Expected SARSA eliminates the randomness from sampling the next action. This typically leads to more stable updates and faster learning.

questions.question: What is the Q-value overestimation problem in Q-Learning?
options: Using max over noisy Q-value estimates systematically biases the target upward because the maximum of noisy estimates is higher than the true maximum
questions.explanation: max_a Q(s',a) takes the maximum over estimates. If estimates have noise (which they do during learning), the maximum will be positively biased — this is the overestimation bias.

questions.question: Why does the max operator in Q-Learning cause overestimation?
options: The discount factor is too large
Noise in Q-value estimates means some estimates are too high by chance, and max selects these overestimates
The learning rate is too small
questions.explanation: With noisy estimates, max systematically selects the most optimistic (highest) estimate. Even if the average estimate is unbiased, the maximum is positively biased — this is a fundamental statistical property.

questions.question: What is 'Double Q-Learning' and how does it solve overestimation?
options: It uses twice the learning rate
It maintains two independent Q-function estimates and uses one to select the action and the other to evaluate it, decoupling selection from evaluation
questions.explanation: Double Q-Learning uses two Q-functions (Q1 and Q2). To update Q1, it uses Q1 to select the best action but Q2 to evaluate it. This decoupling removes the positive bias from using the same function for both selection and evaluation.

questions.question: What is the Double Q-Learning update rule?
options: Q1(s,a) ← Q1(s,a) + α[R + γQ2(s', argmax_a' Q1(s',a')) - Q1(s,a)]
questions.explanation: Double Q-Learning uses Q1 to select the maximizing action (selection), then uses Q2 to evaluate that action (evaluation). The two functions are alternated in updates.

questions.question: What is 'Clipped Double Q-Learning' used in TD3?
options: Taking the minimum of two Q-function estimates for the target to be pessimistic and reduce overestimation bias even further
questions.explanation: Clipped Double Q-Learning (TD3) uses two Q-networks and takes the minimum of their estimates for the target. This pessimistic approach provides a lower-bound estimate, further reducing overestimation.

questions.question: Why is taking the minimum of two Q-estimates in Clipped Double Q-Learning effective?
options: It provides a pessimistic (lower-bound) estimate that counteracts the optimistic bias of Q-Learning's max operator
questions.explanation: If one Q-network overestimates, the other likely won't overestimate the same value by the same amount. Taking the minimum gives a more conservative, less biased estimate.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'action selection' and 'action evaluation' in Q-Learning?
options: Selection is for policies; evaluation is for models
Action selection chooses which action has the highest Q-value; action evaluation computes the Q-value of that chosen action
Selection is stochastic; evaluation is deterministic
questions.explanation: In Q-Learning, max_a Q(s',a) does both: it selects the best action (via argmax) and evaluates it (via the Q-value). This coupling is what causes overestimation bias.

questions.question: How does Double Q-Learning decouple selection from evaluation?
options: It uses the same network for both
It uses one Q-network for selecting the maximizing action and the other Q-network for computing the value of that action
questions.explanation: Double Q-Learning uses Q_A to select: a* = argmax_a Q_A(s',a). Then it uses Q_B to evaluate: target = Q_B(s', a*). This breaks the correlation between selection and evaluation.

questions.question: What is a 'fixed Q-target' in DQN?
options: A target that changes at every step
Using a separate target network with frozen weights to compute the TD target, updated only periodically
questions.explanation: Fixed Q-targets use a separate network (target network) with frozen weights to compute the target R + γ max Q_target(s',a'). This target changes slowly, stabilizing learning.

questions.question: Why do fixed Q-targets help stabilize DQN training?
options: They make the target change faster
They prevent the target from changing at every update, reducing the non-stationarity of the learning objective
questions.explanation: Without fixed targets, the TD target depends on the same network being updated, creating a moving target problem. Fixed targets make the objective more stationary, similar to supervised learning.

questions.question: What is the 'moving target problem' in Q-Learning with neural networks?
options: The agent physically moves too fast
The target network moves between devices
Both the predictions and the targets are computed by the same changing network, causing the goal to shift continuously during training
questions.explanation: In standard Q-Learning, the target R + γ max Q(s',a';θ) uses the same parameters θ being updated. Every update changes θ, which changes the target — like chasing a moving goal.

questions.question: How does using a target network solve the moving target problem?
options: By freezing the target network weights for multiple updates, the target values remain temporarily stable
It makes the state space smaller
questions.explanation: The target network's weights are frozen for N steps. During this time, the main network chases a fixed target, making the optimization problem more like supervised learning.

questions.question: What is 'Double DQN' (DDQN) and how does it differ from DQN?
options: It uses the online network to select actions and the target network to evaluate them, reducing overestimation while keeping the target network
It is a completely different algorithm
questions.explanation: Double DQN modifies DQN by using the online network for action selection (argmax) and the target network for action evaluation. This decoupling reduces overestimation bias.

questions.question: What is the Double DQN target calculation?
questions.explanation: Double DQN uses the online network to select the best action (argmax_a Q_online) and the target network to evaluate that action's Q-value. This is the decoupled selection-evaluation principle.

questions.question: What is 'multi-step learning' or 'n-step Q-Learning'?
options: Using returns computed over n actual steps before bootstrapping: G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + γR_{t+2} + ... + γ^{n-1}R_{t+n} + γ^n max Q(s_{t+n}, a)
questions.explanation: Multi-step learning uses n actual rewards plus a bootstrapped estimate. This trades off between the low variance of TD(1-step) and the low bias of Monte Carlo.

questions.question: What is the advantage of multi-step targets over 1-step TD targets?
options: They eliminate the need for a target network
They propagate real rewards faster and reduce bias from bootstrapping, often leading to faster learning
questions.explanation: Multi-step targets include more actual rewards and less bootstrapping. This gives the algorithm more signal per update and can significantly speed up learning in practice.

questions.question: What is a disadvantage of using very large n in multi-step learning?
options: It requires more memory for the network
Larger n increases variance because the return depends on more random transitions and actions
questions.explanation: While larger n reduces bias, it also includes more randomness from the trajectory, increasing variance. Very large n approaches Monte Carlo, which can be too noisy.

questions.question: What is 'Q-value clipping' and when might it be used?
options: Limiting Q-value estimates to a reasonable range to prevent extreme values that could destabilize training
questions.explanation: Q-value clipping constrains estimates to a fixed range. This prevents occasional extreme TD errors from causing catastrophic updates, especially early in training.

questions.question: What is 'huber loss' and why is it commonly used as the loss function in DQN?
options: It eliminates the need for a target network
It behaves like MSE for small errors (smooth) and MAE for large errors (robust to outliers)
questions.explanation: The Huber loss is quadratic near zero (smooth gradient) and linear far from zero (robust to outliers). In DQN, it prevents large TD errors from causing destructive weight updates.

questions.question: What is 'gradient clipping by norm' in DQN training?
options: Scaling the gradient vector so its L2 norm does not exceed a threshold, preventing very large parameter updates
questions.explanation: If the gradient norm exceeds the threshold, it is rescaled to have that norm. This prevents occasional exploding gradients from causing catastrophic weight changes.

questions.question: What is the 'bias' in TD learning with function approximation?
options: Bias only exists in Monte Carlo
Function approximation introduces approximation error, and bootstrapping can propagate this error, causing biased value estimates
questions.explanation: With function approximation, the true value function may not be exactly representable. TD bootstrapping can propagate these approximation errors, leading to biased estimates.

questions.question: What is 'deadly triad' and which three elements does it combine?
options: Function approximation + bootstrapping + off-policy training, which together can lead to divergence and instability
questions.explanation: The deadly triad is the combination of function approximation, bootstrapping, and off-policy learning. Any two can work together, but all three together can cause learning to diverge.

questions.question: Why can off-policy training with bootstrapping and function approximation diverge?
options: The distribution of states being updated may differ from the distribution the function approximator is optimized for, causing instability
questions.explanation: Off-policy learning updates values for states visited by the behavior policy, but the function approximator generalizes across all states. This distribution mismatch can amplify errors and cause divergence.

questions.question: What is 'off-policy correction' and why is it needed?
options: Adjusting updates to account for the difference between the behavior policy (collecting data) and the target policy (being learned)
questions.explanation: When learning off-policy, the data distribution doesn't match the target policy's distribution. Correction methods (like importance sampling) adjust for this mismatch.

questions.question: What is 'importance sampling ratio' in off-policy TD learning?
options: The ratio of two discount factors
The ratio of two learning rates
ρ = π(A_t|S_t) / b(A_t|S_t), the ratio of target policy probability to behavior policy probability for the taken action
questions.explanation: The importance sampling ratio reweights each transition by how much more or less likely it would be under the target policy compared to the behavior policy.

questions.question: What problem can arise with importance sampling ratios in off-policy learning?
options: The ratios can become very large or very small, causing high variance in the updates
questions.explanation: When the behavior and target policies differ significantly, importance sampling ratios can have extreme values, leading to very high variance in the learning updates.

questions.question: What is 'Tree Backup' in off-policy learning?
options: An off-policy algorithm that uses multi-step returns without importance sampling by backing up through the tree of possible actions
questions.explanation: Tree Backup is an off-policy algorithm that computes multi-step returns by considering all possible actions at each step, weighted by their target policy probabilities, avoiding importance sampling ratios.

questions.question: What is 'Retrace' (λ) in off-policy learning?
options: A method for retracing steps physically
An off-policy algorithm that uses truncated importance sampling ratios with a maximum of 1, combining the low variance of Tree Backup with the efficiency of importance sampling
questions.explanation: Retrace(λ) clips importance sampling ratios at 1. This prevents extreme ratios (reducing variance) while still allowing efficient off-policy learning. It's used in algorithms like IMPALA.

questions.question: What is the advantage of Retrace over standard importance sampling?
options: By capping ratios at 1, it prevents extreme variance while still enabling effective off-policy learning
questions.explanation: Standard importance sampling can have unbounded variance. Retrace caps the ratio at 1, which limits variance while still providing unbiased or low-bias off-policy corrections.

questions.question: What is 'distributional reinforcement learning'?
options: Instead of learning expected values, learning the full distribution of possible returns
questions.explanation: Distributional RL learns the probability distribution of returns rather than just the mean. This captures uncertainty and can lead to more stable learning and better exploration.

questions.question: What is C51 in distributional reinforcement learning?
options: An algorithm that represents the return distribution as a categorical distribution over a fixed set of atoms (51 values), hence C51
questions.explanation: C51 (Categorical 51) discretizes the return distribution into 51 atoms (support points) and learns the probability mass at each atom. This captures the full distribution of possible returns.

questions.question: What advantage does learning the return distribution provide over learning just the expected return?
options: It captures risk and uncertainty information, can improve sample efficiency, and provides richer signal for learning
It eliminates the need for exploration
questions.explanation: Knowing the full distribution (not just the mean) tells us about risk, uncertainty, and the range of possible outcomes. This richer signal often leads to better policies.

questions.question: What is 'Noisy Nets' and how does it help exploration?
options: A network that produces random outputs
Neural networks where the weights include learnable noise parameters, providing state-conditional exploration without ε-greedy
questions.explanation: Noisy Nets replace ε-greedy exploration with learned parameter noise. The network learns how much to explore in each state, often outperforming ε-greedy while eliminating the need to tune ε.

questions.question: How does Noisy Nets differ from adding action noise (like ε-greedy)?
options: Noisy Nets perturb the network parameters, making exploration state-dependent and temporally consistent, rather than adding independent noise to each action
questions.explanation: Action noise (ε-greedy) makes independent random decisions. Noisy Nets perturb weights, causing consistent behavioral changes across similar states, leading to more coherent exploration.

questions.question: What is 'dueling DQN' architecture?
options: Two DQN networks fighting each other
A network architecture that separately estimates state values V(s) and action advantages A(s,a), combining them to get Q-values
A network with double the layers
questions.explanation: Dueling DQN has two streams: one estimates V(s) and the other estimates A(s,a). Q(s,a) = V(s) + A(s,a) - mean(A). This is more efficient when many actions have similar values.

questions.question: Why can the dueling architecture be more sample efficient than standard DQN?
options: It can learn state values without needing to evaluate every action in every update, making learning more efficient when actions have similar values
It uses a larger replay buffer
questions.explanation: In states where all actions have similar values, standard DQN must estimate each Q-value separately. Dueling DQN learns V(s) directly, which transfers to all actions, reducing the learning burden.

questions.question: What is 'Rainbow DQN'?
options: An algorithm that combines six DQN improvements: Double DQN, Prioritized Replay, Dueling Networks, Multi-step learning, Distributional RL, and Noisy Nets
questions.explanation: Rainbow combines the six most impactful DQN extensions into one algorithm. Each component addresses a different limitation, and together they achieve state-of-the-art performance on Atari.

questions.question: Which six components are combined in Rainbow DQN?
options: Double DQN, Prioritized Experience Replay, Dueling Networks, Multi-step Learning, Distributional RL (C51), and Noisy Nets
questions.explanation: Rainbow integrates: (1) Double DQN for less overestimation, (2) PER for better sampling, (3) Dueling for efficient value learning, (4) Multi-step for faster propagation, (5) C51 for distributional learning, (6) Noisy Nets for adaptive exploration.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of combining multiple DQN improvements into Rainbow?
options: To make the algorithm more complex
To address different limitations simultaneously — each component tackles a different problem, and they are largely complementary
To reduce the number of hyperparameters
questions.explanation: Each DQN improvement addresses a different issue (overestimation, sample efficiency, exploration, etc.). Rainbow shows that these improvements are largely independent and combine synergistically.
